title: Soul on Ice
description: An explosive collection of prison essays on race, sexuality, violence, and American power.
descriptionParagraphs: An explosive collection of prison essays on race, sexuality, violence, and American power.

Soul on Ice is organized less as a story than as an argument. As a essay collection, it tries to persuade readers through selection, emphasis, and direct claims about race, prison writing, and radical politics.

Its significance lies in the way it compresses large claims into memorable formulas and positions. Even readers who reject the work usually have to reckon with how sharply it frames questions about race, prison writing, and radical politics.

overview.englishDescription: An explosive collection of prison essays on race, sexuality, violence, and American power.
banningParagraphs: Soul on Ice entered censorship debates as a essay collection associated with race, prison writing, and radical politics. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around racial politics and anti state.

The earliest event currently captured here is late 1960s in South Africa, where Apartheid authorities banned circulation. Cleaver's writing was treated as revolutionary and destabilizing. This entry underscores how governments police imported Black radical writing.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Everybody's Protest Novel
counterReadings.author: James Baldwin
counterReadings.note: A skeptical response to the politics of moral uplift in canonical anti-slavery fiction.

counterReadings.title: The Wretched of the Earth
counterReadings.author: Frantz Fanon
counterReadings.note: Helps contextualize race, violence, and liberation in books targeted under colonial or apartheid systems.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
